segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic title card 'DOUBLE EPISODE' over a dimly lit, ornate hallway, accompanied by tense, dramatic background music. The scene transitions to the formal courtroom of 'Paternity Court' for the 'MANSER v. SEHR, Season 1' case. A commanding female voice (the judge) addresses Bill Manser, a man in a gray suit and purple shirt, who appears solemn and tense at the witness stand. The judge states, "Mr. Manser, you claim you spent five years in prison for failing to pay child support for the defendant Miss Seer's son, Dylan," to which Bill Manser confirms with a resigned "That's right." The judge continues, highlighting that it was "never proven that you are his biological father," again met with Bill Manser's "That's right." A wide shot briefly shows a young man, Dylan, sitting alone and anxious on a hallway bench, as on-screen text asks, 'Is Bill Manser Dylan's biological father?' The judge then turns to Elizabeth Sehr, a woman in a vibrant magenta blazer, stating, "Now Miss Seer, you say you have proof that Mr. Manser is Dylan's father," and that she is there "to finally stop him running from his responsibility." Elizabeth, appearing composed yet earnest, responds with a polite "Yes, ma'am." The courtroom is shown in full, with Bill and Elizabeth facing each other across the blue-carpeted floor, under the watchful eyes of the audience, as on-screen text reiterates the conflict: 'Five years in prison for unpaid support—but is he the father?' The judge then instructs Bill Manser to "explain to the court the circumstances surrounding your arrest." Alternating close-ups show Bill, now appearing defensive and slightly agitated, as he begins to explain in a clear, somewhat resigned voice, "Your Honor, I just I'm here to explain a 12 hours of my life cost me five years in Department of Corrections." He continues, detailing his past connection to Elizabeth, stating, "She was a friend of my aunt... We all lived in the same little community. She lived across the street. I knew of her, I knew her, I've spoken to her," all while the tense background music persists, reinforcing the gravity of the legal proceedings.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Judge, a woman in a black robe with a red collar and turquoise necklace, seated at the bench, gesturing emphatically as she speaks, setting a serious tone. The scene quickly shifts to Bill Manser, a middle-aged man with graying hair in a gray suit, purple shirt, and patterned tie, standing at the witness stand, appearing tense and defensive as he gestures while speaking. A female voice, identified as Elizabeth Sehr, immediately questions Bill, asking "So how did you get arrested?" Bill, speaking passionately and defensively, explains his arrest came three years after he still hadn't paid child support, stating he was "still unable to pay" and had "doubt" about paternity, claiming he'd "never know for sure whether or not I was the natural father." Elizabeth, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair in a bright magenta blazer, stands composed and hopeful beside him, asserting he "would have known" if he'd shown up for court. Bill counters he "couldn't show up for court," leading to a heated exchange where Elizabeth accuses him of not paying child support for "about eight years," correcting his claim of three. On-screen text reveals Elizabeth claims Bill owes $17,000 for 20-year-old Dylan, while Bill's text states he spent five years in prison for a child he claims isn’t his. Bill continues, detailing his prison sentence for five years and subsequent child support payments after his release in 2003, which Elizabeth quickly interjects were "only because they garnished your wages." The camera cuts to a split-screen image showing two young men labeled 'BILL' and 'DYLAN,' suggesting a visual comparison for the paternity dispute, as the audience, seated in wooden pews, watches intently with expressions of concern and judgment. Bill, appearing increasingly agitated in close-ups, passionately demands proof, stating he's "yet to see proof that the child's mine," and recounts his public defender's explanation of DNA being obtained from an existing sample, which Elizabeth attempts to counter. A wide shot shows the courtroom layout, including the Judge and a police officer, as a graphic overlay appears with the text 'FIVE YEARS IN PRISON FOR UNPAID SUPPORT - BUT IS HE THE FATHER?' The segment concludes with Bill and Elizabeth standing at the witness stand, facing each other amidst their tense exchange, observed by the audience.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Bill Manser, a middle-aged man with graying hair, wearing a gray suit, purple shirt, and patterned tie, appearing tense and defensive as he speaks directly to the camera. On-screen text identifies Bill and states his claim: he was never proven to be the biological father of Elizabeth’s son. Bill continues, arguing that it's "cruel and unusual punishment to be sentenced to five years in prison with rapists and and murderers and and and child molesters... for missing a court date, because that's basically what happened. I missed a court date." Elizabeth challenges him again, "You missed being a father," to which Bill defensively replies, "I didn't know he was mine." The scene cuts to a wide shot of the courtroom, showing Bill at a witness stand facing Elizabeth Sehr, who is dressed in a bright magenta blazer over a black top, standing at her own stand with a composed demeanor. Judge Lenna Lake, seated behind a wooden bench adorned with American and state flags, listens intently, gesturing as she speaks. The Judge, with a firm, authoritative voice, then interjects, "Well, once you don't show up for court... Mr. Manser, yeah... you understand that now, that in this country, you got to show up for court. If you got to walk there." Bill concedes, "You're right. The atmosphere is tense, with the judge’s questioning and Bill’s defensive posture heightening the drama. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Bill Manser, a middle-aged man in a gray suit, purple shirt, and patterned tie, appearing tense and defensive as he speaks emphatically in the courtroom, arguing a hypothetical scenario about paternity and travel, stating, "way out there that goes to Florida on spring break and hooks up with a girl, and the girl goes to Texas, and the boy goes back to Massachusetts." The Judge, a woman in a black robe, interjects authoritatively, challenging Bill's argument and stating, "If he can't fly from Massachusetts to Texas, he's on automatically the natural father," adding emphatically, "He better find a way." As the Judge tries to regain control, saying "listen, listen," she addresses Bill, acknowledging, "The pain that you've endured and what you've gone through is in part because..." Bill interjects, admitting, "My stubbornness," which the Judge affirms, adding, "And you are a living example of that." Bill again interjects, asserting, "And it needs to be known out there." The Judge then recounts Bill's past stance, quoting him, "So now as you're being hauled off to court, you're still adamant and you're saying, 'I'm not the father of this child.'" Bill clarifies, "I'm saying I've never been proven to be the father of this child," and adds a new detail, "I hadn't met him until he was like 15 years after I initiated contact for my son Chris." The Judge interjects with "So wait," before asking Bill directly, "Now are you convinced he is the father?" The camera cuts to Elizabeth Sehr, a woman in a bright magenta blazer, standing composed at the witness stand, who responds to the Judge, "Yes, ma'am." When asked "Why?" by the Judge, Elizabeth explains, "Because I have had a DNA test done and I have the results right here, would you like a copy of it?" Bill quickly interjects, attempting to discredit her, stating, "She's worked for lab pretty much for the last 20..." A wide-angle shot earlier revealed a young man in a dark suit, appearing anxious and contemplative, with on-screen text stating, 'FIVE YEARS IN PRISON FOR UNPAID SUPPORT - BUT IS HE THE FATHER?' The segment concludes with a split-screen graphic comparing side-by-side photos labeled 'BILL' and 'DYLAN,' visually assessing potential physical similarities, heightening the narrative tension of the paternity dispute. I mean she could get those anyway." The Judge, a woman in a black robe, interjects authoritatively, "Hold on one second. Jerome, please hand me Miss Seer's evidence." A uniformed officer, addressed as Jerome, walks toward the judge’s bench, handing her a document, with a brief, neutral male voice confirming, "There you go." Bill Manser continues his defensive argument, explaining that "the seal was not tampered with therefore I could not have tampered with the sample," and asserts, "I never submitted a test. I got a hold of court clerks. They sent me every I have a docket sheet here that pretty much details everything that's ever taken place in my file. There's nothing in there that shows any reliable results." He then makes a derogatory comment about Elizabeth's past, mentioning "she had a boyfriend at the time, she she had a not a ho Indian." The Judge firmly interrupts, correcting him, "Jerome, that was her boyfriend. Please hand me Mr. Manser's evidence as well." Elizabeth Sehr, sounding surprised, questions, "He has evidence of what?" Bill Manser quickly responds, "I have evidence that there was never a DNA test. There's no DNA test in there." A promotional voice-over then encourages viewers to "subscribe and click on the notification bell" for more episodes of Paternity Court, accompanied by a dramatic sound effect. The scene returns to the courtroom as the Judge, maintaining her firm, authoritative demeanor, raises her hand and states, "Before we continue to argue this, let me look at Miss Seer's evidence that she included because..." as Bill Manser looks on with a mix of anxiety and defiance, and the audience displays varied expressions, underscoring the tense atmosphere of the paternity dispute.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Judge, seated behind her bench amidst American and state flags, speaking authoritatively as she questions the situation surrounding a previously collected DNA sample. Bill Manser, a middle-aged man in a gray suit, appears tense at the defendant's table, defensively interjecting, "Who's to say it was my sample? May they got it mixed up with John Smith down the street, you know." The Judge firmly states that the evidence clearly indicates a laboratory test was performed, to which Elizabeth Sehr, composed in a magenta blazer at the witness stand, calmly responds, "Yes, ma'am." Bill then accuses Elizabeth, claiming, "I don't think it happened. I think she worked in a lab and she's had this thing manufactured." The Judge, with a hint of sarcasm, remarks, "This is what movies are made of," prompting Elizabeth to humorously retort, "I am good at my job, but I'm not that good." The Judge directly asks Elizabeth if she worked at a lab, to which Bill attempts to interrupt. Elizabeth clarifies, explaining she worked at a plasma center harvesting plasma, emphasizing it "had nothing to do with DNA." Bill persists with a slightly insinuating tone, suggesting that in business, one knows others in the same field. A wide shot shows Bill and Elizabeth facing the Judge, with the concerned and curious Courtroom Audience in the background. The Judge then reviews documents, and a graphic overlay appears, posing the question 'FIVE YEARS IN PRISON FOR UNPAID SUPPORT - BUT IS HE THE FATHER?' The scene concludes with a close-up of a DNA test form, featuring handwritten entries and Elizabeth's photo, as the Judge deliberately begins to detail the information included in the sample collection.

segments.integrated_caption: The courtroom of 'Paternity Court' is charged with tension under warm, dramatic lighting. Bill Manser, a middle-aged man in a gray suit, stands defensively at the defendant’s table, his voice heard stating, "You're the grandpa," followed by Elizabeth Sehr, in a bright magenta blazer at the witness stand, expressing hope, "I'd like for you to be part of her life too." Bill then defensively advises, "I've advised you to get proof," to which Elizabeth retorts with a strong, accusatory tone, "I've advised him to raise his child from day one," drawing applause from the intently watching Courtroom Audience. Bill reiterates, "Once you get proof, you will." The Judge, authoritative behind her bench, addresses Elizabeth, noting, "Miss Seer, you are very emotional right now. What are you feeling in this moment?" Elizabeth, visibly emotional and touching her face, passionately declares, "I feel like I have never ever deserved disrespect. I was not the woman that showed up at your doorstep every two months, 'You're going to be my baby's daddy.' I have never disrespected you. I let you be and live your life and be live your selfish life you have lived for the last 20 years," which again elicits applause from the audience. The Judge then announces, "I think it's time for the results," and Elizabeth confirms, "Yes, ma'am," followed by more applause. The Judge calls for "Jerome?" and asks the Uniformed Officer, a man in a light blue uniform who walks across the courtroom, "Do you have the envelope?" The officer confirms, "I do," and adds, "Everything's in there." The Judge thanks him, and he replies, "You're welcome." The Judge then begins to read the DNA test results, stating, "These results were prepared by DNA Diagnostics and because this is a very special case and we've had previous DNA testing done in which our plaintiff questions..." building suspense as the segment ends.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Judge, a woman with dark hair in a black robe over a red-collared shirt, seated behind a wooden bench flanked by flags, speaking authoritatively. Jasmine Johnson, 24, in a yellow and gray chevron-patterned top with braided hair, appears anxious as she speaks, stating the situation is "bringing problems with us and with me, my mother-in-law." The Judge then questions Walter Johnson, 27, in a light blue shirt and gray vest, asking how this is affecting his marriage. Walter, initially anxious, responds that he feels they'll have "a lot more closure if I go ahead and find out if they're mine or not." The Judge presses him, stating he's a "married man and you're torn between two women now." Walter replies, "Basically, but now I'm not gonna say now because me and Ms. Davis don't have any more dealings." The Judge challenges him, asking if she's "still wreaking havoc on your relationship," to which Walter, now appearing more composed, says he's "not gonna necessarily say that." The Judge demands, "What are you gonna say?" Walter explains that not knowing "if the kids are mine, yeah, that's causing havoc," because his wife won't "get any rest at all if she got to wonder if I got other kids outside of us." The Judge asks who's responsible, and Walter admits, "Me, I take full responsibility." The Judge urges him to "Say something then. That's what I'm talking about. Let's say something, let's tell the truth," before summarizing, "So the bottom line is you potentially have children outside your marriage." Walter confirms with a "Yeah." The screen splits to show Jasmine, Walter, and Senner Davis, 36, all looking serious and focused. A graphic then displays three photos: a baby labeled 'CHRISTIANA,' a man labeled 'MR. JOHNSON,' and another baby labeled 'CHRISTIAN,' set against a blue background. The scene returns to Jasmine and Walter, who exchange glances, then cuts back to the Judge, who is gesturing. Walter smiles slightly. The camera pulls back to a wide shot showing Jasmine and Walter at one witness stand, and Senner Davis at another, with Laura Johnson, 46, standing beside her. Senner Davis then speaks, stating, "One minute they're his, then the next minute they're..." in the formal courtroom setting with wooden paneling, blue carpet, and a large window, creating a tense and dramatic atmosphere as the paternity case unfolds.
